You say yes. Another silence. "Mosst humansss do not know. Mosst humansss... do not arrrive."

It thinks about this. Then: "You come with usss. We decide what to do with you when we arrrive."

exit.travel_text: They lead you through passages you'd never have found alone. The tunnels widen. Firelight flickers ahead.

steps.text: You fall in behind them at a distance, keeping to the shadows.

exit.travel_text: They lead you through a series of low tunnels, unaware. The passages widen. Firelight appears ahead.

steps.text: You make it maybe thirty paces before one of them stops, turns, and looks directly at you. There's a moment where everyone pretends this hasn't happened. Then it doesn't pretend anymore.

A knot of Wisps drifts through the tunnel ahead — fire spirits, or what's left of them. They move in slow, looping circles, like they've forgotten where they were going. The heat off them is noticeable even at twenty paces.

watch.text: They drift past without noticing you. After a minute they disappear into a crack in the rock too narrow to follow. The air cools slightly. You move on.

follow.text: You fall in behind them. It's surprisingly calming — they drift without urgency and the heat isn't unpleasant. They lead you through two side passages and stop abruptly in a small alcove, hovering around something on the ground. A cracked leather satchel, charred around the edges. Inside: something useful.

fight.text: You wade in swinging. They're faster than they look.

steps.text: You stop in front of the largest panel of obsidian. Your reflection stares back — warped, too dark, wrong somehow. It's a half-second behind you. Then a full second. It stops moving before you do.

The glass is warm and faintly vibrating. Your reflection presses its hand to yours from the other side. You stay there for a moment. Then it blinks — and it's just your reflection again, moving when you move. Nothing else happens.
The glass is warm and faintly vibrating. For a moment you feel — steadied. Like something lined up inside you. You pull your hand back, oddly refreshed.
The glass is scalding. You pull back immediately, the skin on your fingertips raw. Your reflection looks fine.

leave.text: You don't look at it again. Probably wise.

smash.text: The obsidian shatters into razor-edged shards. Your reflection disappears mid-expression. One catches your arm on the way out.

steps.text: Something protrudes from the ash field — a dark shape, half-buried, at about waist height. You get closer. It's a weapon handle, the blade driven straight down.

choices.choice: Leave it — this is someone's grave

steps.text: It comes free with effort — a Battleaxe, old but solid, the blade scorched but intact. No grave marker. No bones visible. You keep it.
It comes free, but the handle crumbles in your grip — too old, too far gone. The blade hits the ash and splits. Nothing left worth keeping.
It comes free. A Falchion — decent condition. Then the ash moves. Something down there was holding the other end.

leave.text: You walk around it and move on. The handle is still there in the ash behind you.

steps.text: There's a narrow path along the magma channel's edge — crumbling in places, but it would cut significant time off your route. The alternative is the long way around.
You move fast and keep your eyes ahead, not down. The ledge holds. You cross in under a minute, the magma roar fading behind you.
Midway, a section of ledge gives way. You keep your footing, but your leg dips into the superheated air directly above the flow. The burn is immediate.

safe.text: You go around. It takes longer, but your legs are unburned.

steps.text: At the back of the shaft, propped against the wall like it was left between shifts: a pickaxe. Ancient, heavy, short-handled, the head still solid. The rock face opposite has fresh scoring and a few glittering seams left half-exposed. Whoever left this wasn't done.
The pickaxe is heavier than it looks. You set your stance against the rock face and start working the seams.

The rock doesn't yield. You're forcing the angle wrong and you know it, but keep going anyway. The pickaxe handle cracks, the head pings off into the dark, and you're left with a splinter in your palm and nothing to show for it.
[color=red]-5 HP[/color]
The seam runs out fast. You chase it deeper, angle after angle, until the pickaxe head snaps clean off and bounces away into the dark. You stand there with a broken handle. Nothing.
The rock gives nothing. A dry swing, another, a third. No vein, no pocket, nothing but dust. The pickaxe is still in one piece, at least.

You hit something. Using your hands, you try pulling it out.[wait=0.1] Jackpot! It's big gold nugget, as big as your fist! You're rich!

...Or not. Upon closer inspection, it's actually fools' gold.
You got [color=yellow]40G[/color] (eventually).
You find the right angle and the rock splits. A small pocket — rough gems, uncut, catching the torchlight at odd angles. Small, but real, and sure to be worth something.
You got [color=yellow]100G[/color] (eventually).
The wall gives suddenly — a vein of gold, running wide. More than you expected. By the time you tally it all, your wallet feels a lot heavier.
You found [color=yellow]150G[/color].
You break a seam and something catches the torchlight. Jackpot! Amber — pale orange, warm even underground. Something preserved inside, an insect you don't recognise. You pocket it.
You break a seam and something catches the torchlight that isn't ore. Jackpot! A ruby, deep red, still warm from the stone around it. The Core Cavern's warmth, stored for centuries. You pocket it.
You break a seam and something catches the torchlight that isn't ore. Jackpot! Obsidian — volcanic glass, still sharp where it broke clean. You feel more dangerous just by holding it. You pocket it.

The pickaxe hits something that doesn't sound like rock — a hollow, crystalline ring. You clear around it with your hands. A glowing crystal, embedded in the wall. It pulses when you touch it, reminding you of the Caves of Light.
The pickaxe is starting to feel the work. The head is still solid, but the handle's warming in your grip.

continue.text: You reset your stance and drive the pickaxe into the next seam.

steps.text:
The rock fights back. A bad angle, a bad swing — the handle bites into your palm and the impact jars up your arm badly enough to make you drop it. You stand there bleeding, pickaxe at your feet.
[color=red]-5 HP[/color]
You find nothing as you strike the seam, even as the pickaxe head wobbles loose and drops. That's the end of that.
Nothing. The rock is stubborn here — or there's nothing to find. The pickaxe is holding, barely.

stop.text: You set the pickaxe down against the rock face and find the main shaft.

steps.text: The pickaxe is down to its last few swings. You can feel it.

continue.text: You drive the pickaxe into the rock one last time.

steps.text:
One swing too many. The handle splits and the head skips off the rock into your knee. You limp back from the face, bleeding, the broken handle still in your fist.
[color=red]-5 HP[/color]
Nothing here. The seam runs cold, and the pickaxe gives out on the last swing — head spinning off into the dark. You set the broken handle against the wall and walk away.
Nothing in this stretch. The pickaxe gives out on the last swing, handle splitting down the grain. You set it down and call it.
The pickaxe is done.
The pickaxe is done.
The pickaxe is done.
The pickaxe is done.
The pickaxe is done.
The pickaxe is done.

stop.text: You set the pickaxe down against the rock face and walk away. It was probably too dangerous to swing, anyway.

steps.text: A Drake is wedged into a side passage, making a sound somewhere between a growl and a wheeze. One of its legs is badly burned — lava splash, probably. It's not going anywhere. But it's watching you with the focused attention of something that can absolutely still bite.

choices.choice: Put it out of its misery

steps.text: You move slowly, hands open. It flinches when you touch the burned leg but doesn't bite. You do what you can — some cloth, wrapped tight. When you step back, it lets out a slow, low exhale. Later, in Lizal-Town, a Lizal catches your eye and places one hand flat against their chest, briefly, then looks away.

You move too fast. It lunges — more scared than vicious — and bites down on your arm before you can pull back. The Drake scrambles deeper into the passage. You stand there bleeding, reconsidering your approach to wildlife.

leave.text: You back out carefully. The Drake's eyes track you the whole way. You leave it to work things out.

fight.text: It doesn't go down easy, wounded leg and all.

on_victory.text: You stand over it. The passage is quiet now.

steps.text: Something hits the line. Not a nibble — a yank. The rod bends nearly double.
You brace your feet against the bank and pull back. Whatever's down there is pulling harder.

A shape rises beneath the surface — wide as a barrel, mud-dark. A head the size of a cooking pot breaks the water. It stares at you with one flat, indifferent eye.

The biggest catfish you've ever seen. By a considerable margin.

choices.choice: Cut the line and back away.

fight.text: You dig your heels in. The catfish surges and splashes. You're not sure how long you can hold.

cut.text: You pull your knife and sever the line. The rod springs back. The shape drops beneath the surface without a sound.
The biggest fish you've ever seen, and you let it go. Probably for the best.

steps.text: You spot someone up ahead.

A weathered man sits at a stump by the roadside, three carved bone dice rolling idly between his fingers. He looks up as you approach.

"Care for a game? Wanderer's Dice — oldest game on the road."
"Now, you up for another game, kid?"

on_win.text:
Your hand takes the pot. He slides the coins across without complaint.

"Well played."

on_tie.text: A dead match. He pushes your stake back without a word.

on_fold.text:
You lay your dice down. He sweeps the pot to his side of the stump.

"Smart. Sometimes folding is the only play."

on_lose.text:
He lays his hand flat on the stump and sweeps the coins off with practiced calm.

"Better luck next time, friend."

on_win.text:
Your hand takes the pot. He counts out the coins and sets them in front of you.

"You've played before."

on_tie.text: Neither hand better. He returns your stake without a word.

on_fold.text:
You fold. He nods once and pulls the pot to his side.

"No shame in knowing when to walk."

on_lose.text:
He adds your coins to his purse with a dry smile.

"The bones decide, not me."

then.text:
Your hand takes the pot. Your opponent stares at the dice for a long time — longer than he's looked at you the whole game.

"...Good game."

"You've got gumption, kid. Take this, too: Someone with that kind of nerve deserves a better die."

It's a carved bone die, just a bit heavier than it should be. The secret to Traveller's Dice is in the dice you've got, see? And sometimes, you need something with more... consistent results."

The travelling gambler smiles.

"You'll encounter all sorts dice people use in road, some fairer than others, so you've stack things as far in your favor as you can, see?"

"Make sure to check the rules often, and keep your mouth shut about what you've got, and you're gonna go far, kid."

else.text:
Your hand takes the pot. The man stares at the dice for a long moment, then smiles.

"...Good game, kid."

on_tie.text: Your opponent studies the table. Pushes back your stake. "...Good game."

on_fold.text:
You fold. Your opponent pulls the pot to his side with both hands.

"High stakes. Wise choice."

on_lose.text:
He pulls the coins to his side of the stump slowly.

"High stakes, high lessons. Never gamble what you don't want Lady Luck to keep."

leave.text: "Another time, then." He goes back to rolling the dice between his fingers as you walk away.

info.text:
He leans back and rolls one of the dice across his knuckles.

"Simple enough. Each of us has three private dice. Two more go in the middle — Dealer's dice — and those belong to the table. You pick any five from the lot to build your best hand."

He lines three dice on the stump.

"Three chances to swap one of yours out. After the first two rounds, a middle die gets turned face-up — changes what you can work with. Third round done, everybody shows what they've got."

"Pairs, runs, threes-of-a-kind — runs beat pairs, threes beat runs. Longer runs beat shorter ones. There are special faces too, once you get fancier dice."

"Full list of hands is right there during play, so no need to memorize anything. Lady Luck handles the rest."

The travelling gambler leans back and offers you a die.

"So? Interested in trying it out?"

steps.text: You spot something up ahead:"

Among the headstones, a half-buried figure hunches over a flat-topped grave marker. Three dice on the stone in front of it. It doesn't have eyes anymore, but the empty sockets turn toward you when you approach.

"Sit," it says, in a voice like air through old stone. "One game of Wandering Dice. That is all I ask."

on_win.text:
Your hand takes it. He holds still for a moment, then counts out the coins and sets them in front of you.

"...Well played." He reaches behind the headstone and sets a die on the stone between you. Small, carved from bone, every face the same mark. "Keep it. Something to remember the game by."

on_tie.text:
A dead match. He divides the pot exactly and sets your half in front of you without a word. Then he reaches behind the headstone and places a small die on the stone between you. Every face carved with the same sign.

"Even hands. Take this anyway."

on_fold.text:
You set your dice down. He pulls the pot to his side with careful hands.

"Wise, perhaps." He doesn't look up from the stone. "Come back when you are ready."

on_lose.text:
He sweeps the coins to his side slowly. Then reaches behind the headstone and sets a small bone die on the grave marker between you. Every face carved with the same sign.

"The game leaves something behind," he says. "It always does."

leave.text:
You back away from the grave. The skeleton watches you go, then looks back down at its dice.

It doesn't call after you.

then.text:
"Step right up, step right up!"

Perched atop a garish red-and-purple booth, a short figure in a porcelain mask — one half grinning, the other frowning — throws their arms wide as you approach.

"You. Yes, you. You've got the look of someone who to wants to have some fun!"

They vault off the booth and land in a crouch that shouldn't quite work, then straighten up, with too much energy.

"Welcome — welcome — to the Games of the Gilded Hollow." They spread their arms wide. "Games of strength! Games of skill! Games of wit and luck and more!"

"Play the games! Seek victory! And who knows — maybe you'll walk away with something Fabulous!" [font_size=-2]"Nobody ever has. But I do like to say it."[/font_size]

steps.text: "Back again." They spot you before you're halfway to the booth. "Of course you are. They always come back."

They see you coming from across the grounds. The mask tilts sideways.

"Oh, it's you." A pause. "Feeling lucky today? Or just feeling generous?"
"Back for more, are we?" The clown claps twice, sharply, and drops into a bow. "Splendid. Truly."

The mask turns toward you before you've said a word.

"You again." They clap once. "Good, good! I was starting to get bored."
"Ah!" They point as you approach. "My favorite — no, I say that to everyone." They lower the finger. "But you. You're actually starting to grow on me. Or maybe not. Who knows?"
The calliope grinds through a half-familiar melody somewhere deeper in the grounds.
A cheer rises from further in the circus. Then it stops, cleanly.
One of the harlequins drifts past behind the booth, white mask blank, not looking at you.
The smell of fried oil and sawdust hangs heavy around the stand.
From somewhere behind the tent, something shifts in its cage and then goes still.
"So." They point at you. "What'll it be?"
They lean on the counter and tap it twice. "What'll it be?"
"Don't be shy." They wave a hand. "Pick your poison."
They stare. The mask grins. And frowns. "Well?"
"Take your time." They drum their fingers on the counter. "I'm not going anywhere. Neither are you, really."

steps.header: What would you like to do?

steps.text: "Wonderful!" They clap once. "What would you like to play?"
"That's the spirit!" The clown leans over the counter, mask getting uncomfortably close. "We're going to have so much fun together..."
They spread their arms wide. "Step right up! Which trial will you challenge, this time?"

steps.header: What would you like to play?

steps.text: "The Trial of Wit." The Gamer Clown spins around and faces you. "Good choice! I've been waiting for someone to pick Wit."
They stop. Tilt their head sideways. "Wit," they repeat, slowly. "Interesting choice." One side of the mask grins wider, the other frowns harder. Or maybe it always looked like that.
"Ah, the brainy type." They tap the side of their mask. "Most go for one of the others first. Not you. I respect that. It won't help you, but I respect it."
"Lame." They turn away. "You're lame. Absolutely lame. Do something."

They stare at you for a long moment.

"...You're funny." The mask tilts. "What do you want to do now?"
"Changed your mind?" They shrug with their whole body. "Fine. Back to the start, back to the start. We've got all the time in the world."

then.text: "Entry fee: one hundred gold." They hold out a hand. "You win, you get it back. You lose..." The mask grins. "Well. You had fun."

else.text: "Standard entry is one hundred gold," they say, then hold up a finger. "But — since it's your first time — the first game's on the house. Ten gold. A steal. Don't tell the management." A beat. "Or am I the management? It's been so long, sometimes I forget."

steps.text: You got [color=yellow]100G[/color].

then.text: From beneath the counter, they produce a small vial and slide it over. "A little something for the road."
"Getting good at this." They toss you a vial that fizzes faintly on its own. "Don't drink it out of curiosity."
"Three in a row." They produce a shimmering vial, careful not to touch it directly. "Careful with that one."
"Four wins." The mask tilts, almost impressed. They press a peculiar periwinkle die into your palm — it hums faintly. "That one likes you. Try not to lose it."
"Five!" They produce a scroll from absolutely nowhere and hand it over with a flourish. "Learn a little something of mine."
"Six wins." For a moment, the mask stops moving entirely. Then they set a faintly glowing crystal on the counter. "...That one's not really a prize. Take care of it."
"Seven." They go very quiet, then produce a long coiled whip from behind the booth — one that definitely wasn't there a moment ago. "...The boss won't miss this. Probably."
"Eight!" Another scroll appears between their fingers, like it was always going to. "One more trick, on the house."
"Nine wins." They produce a small dented bell and turn it over in their hands before setting it down. "This one rings when it wants to. Not before."

"Ten." The clown goes utterly still. Then, slowly, they lift something from beneath the booth: a small, tarnished crown, sized for someone with a mask instead of a face. "...Nobody's gotten this far before. Truly. Wear it well."

steps.text: "The Trial of Strength..." The clown pauses. "Ah — that ring's still being set up, actually. Very embarrassing." They slide your coins back across the counter. "On the house. Come back another time."
The mask goes very still. "Every dart, dead center, every single time." They set the darts down slowly. "No room for anything less. Go on, then. Impress me."
"You again." They don't bother with the board this time — just point at the tiny bullseye, alone against the frame. "You know what's needed. Let's see it."
For a long moment, they say nothing at all. Then, quietly: "Perfect or nothing. Same as always." They don't even bother explaining anymore.
They don't bother swapping the board — it's already impossibly small, the fifteen-ring no wider than a fingertip. "Barely any room for error left. Show me."
"Four." The mask doesn't move for a moment. "Nobody gets this far. You understand that, right?" They hand over the darts anyway.
They set the smallest board yet against the frame without comment — just a long look at you before stepping back.
The board they hang this time is barely bigger than a dinner plate, every ring crowded close together. They step back, arms crossed. "Go on, then."
"Three wins." They exhale through the mask, a strange, hollow sound. "Alright. Let's make this interesting." The board shrinks again.
They pin up a board so small you can barely make out the rings from here. "Still feeling confident?"
They squint at you and swap the board again — smaller still, the bullseye barely a coin's width. They toss you the darts. "Let's see if that was luck."
"Two in a row." They tap the mask, thoughtful. "Either you're good, or I'm unlucky. Let's find out which."
The board shrinks again, rings crowding the center. "Getting harder to be impressed," they say, not sounding impressed at all.
"Round two." They swap the target board for a smaller one, rings drawn tighter. "Should still be easy for someone as talented as you." The mask's grin looks unconvinced.
They produce a fresh board, noticeably smaller than the last, and pin it up without a word. "Go on. Surprise me again."
"Back for another round?" They twirl a dart between two fingers. "Let's see if the first one was a fluke."
They reach under the counter and produce three darts, each fletched in mismatched circus colors. "Simple enough." They set a battered target board upright behind the booth.
"Darts!" They pull three of them from behind their back with a flourish, spinning one between gloved fingers. "Nothing to it. Watch — no, wait, you throw. I watch."
They produce three darts and a battered target board in the same motion, as if they'd been holding both the whole time. "Three throws. That's all it takes."
Score [var="circus_skill_threshold"] or better, and you win.
[var="circus_skill_threshold"] points. That's the number to beat.
You'll need [var="circus_skill_threshold"] or higher this time.
Three darts thrown. Final score: [var="circus_skill_score"].
That's all three darts. Total: [var="circus_skill_score"].
The last dart settles. Final tally: [var="circus_skill_score"].
The dart sails wide and thunks into the wooden frame. Zero.
It clatters off the board entirely and rolls under the booth. Zero points.
The clown clicks their tongue. "Ohh. That one doesn't count. That one very much doesn't count." Zero.
The dart bounces off the board and lands at your feet. The clown covers their mask with both hands, shoulders shaking with what might be laughter.
The dart catches the outer ring, barely. Two points.
It lands wobbling on the very edge of the board. Two.
"Two," the clown announces, unimpressed. "Technically a hit. Barely."
The dart clings to the outermost ring like it's not sure it wants to be there. Two points.
A clean hit, low on the board. Four points.
The dart sinks into the middle ring, unremarkable. Four.
"Four," the clown says, already looking elsewhere. "Fine. Adequate."
It thunks into the board with a satisfying little thud. Four points, nothing special.
Closer to center now. Six points.
The dart lands with a solid thunk, well inside the board. Six.
The clown raises an eyebrow — or the mask's painted approximation of one. "Six. Not bad."
It sinks in with confidence. Six points, and the clown actually nods.
Nearly dead center. Eight points.
The dart buries itself just shy of the bullseye. Eight.
"Ooh." The clown leans in. "Eight. Now we're talking."
It lands with a sharp crack, inches from the center. Eight points.
Dead on target. Ten points.
The dart strikes true, right on the mark. Ten.
The clown claps once, sharp. "Ten! Look at that."
It hits with a satisfying thunk, right where it should. Ten points, and the mask actually grins wider.
The dart splits the bullseye clean down the middle. Fifteen!
It buries itself so precisely in the center that the board actually creaks. Fifteen — the mask actually stops moving for a second.
"FIFTEEN!" The clown's voice cracks with something that might be genuine delight. "Do that again. No — don't. It's unsettling."
Dead. Center. The clown stares at the board, then at you, then back at the board. "...Fifteen," they say faintly.
"Well, well." The mask tilts, impressed despite itself. "Look who can actually aim." They sweep the coins back across the counter.
They clap, slow and deliberate. "Not bad. Not bad at all." The prize vanishes and reappears in your hands before you can blink.
"A winner!" They spread their arms. "Give this one a round of applause — oh wait, nobody's watching. Never mind. Well done, anyway."
"Hah!" The clown throws their hands up. "Fine, FINE. You win. Don't let it go to your head."
The mask tilts, considering you for a long moment. "...Good throw." They almost sound sincere.
They collect the darts, unbothered. "Close. Not close enough, though." The mask grins wider. "Try again?"
"Ohh, so close." They don't sound sincere. "The board's rigged, probably. Definitely not you."
They pocket the coins with a shrug. "Practice makes perfect. Allegedly."
"So close!" The clown doesn't sound sorry at all. "Really. So, so close."
They sweep the darts off the counter without a word, mask unreadable.
"Another round?" They're already resetting the board. "Hundred gold, same as always."
They tap the darts against the counter. "Go again? Hundred gold, quick as you like."
"Rematch?" The mask tilts, waiting. "Hundred gold, right now, if you're feeling brave."
"Going again?" They're already lining up a fresh board. "Hundred gold. You know the drill."
The clown spins a dart between two fingers, waiting. "Well? Hundred gold says you can't do it twice."
"Perfect score," the clown says flatly. "That's what I need from you now. Five for five. Impossible questions, trick questions, no easy ones left. This is the real Trial of Wit."
"You've beaten me enough times that I've stopped being nice about it." They fan out a final, ominous-looking deck. "Five out of five. Good luck. You'll need it."
"Four wins." The mask stops moving for a second. "Fine. FINE. Have the really nasty questions. Some worldbuilding you couldn't possibly know yet. Some tricks. Good luck."
"You're really doing this to me," they mutter, shuffling in a set of cards that seem to glow faintly with malice. "Four of five. Go on, then."
"No more easy ones," the clown announces, almost offended. "You've earned yourself the real questions. Still need four of five, though."
"Alright, alright." They crack their gloved knuckles. "Let's see if you actually know things, or if you just got lucky."
"Ooh, someone's on a streak." The mask grins wider. "Four out of five now. And I'm throwing in a few... special questions." They wink. Or seem to. Hard to tell with the mask.
"Getting good, aren't you." They produce a card with a suspiciously smug flourish. "Some of these might not have answers at all. Have fun with that."
"Round two of the brains department," the clown says, shuffling the cards. "Slightly trickier questions this time. Still just need three of five."
"You again." They tilt their head. "Fine. Let's raise the bar — just a little."
"The Trial of Wit!" The Gamer Clown produces a stack of cards from nowhere and fans them out dramatically. "Five questions. Three right, and you win. Simple as that — for now."
"Ohhh, a thinker." They tap their mask. "Let's see what's rattling around in there."
"Five questions!" they announce to nobody in particular. "Get three right and you'll walk away a winner. Or a very lucky guesser. I won't judge — much."
"...Huh." The clown stares at the card in their hand. "You actually got that many right." They toss the coins back across the counter. "Unbelievable. Genuinely."
"Well, color me impressed." They clap, slow and theatrical. "Somebody's been paying attention out there."
"Ugh, fine, you win." They slide the coins over with two fingers, looking personally wounded by it.
"Ding ding ding!" They ring a little bell that appears from nowhere. "We have a winner! An annoyingly smart one!"
"You know things." The mask tilts, unreadable. "I don't love that about you."
"Ohhh, so close." They sweep the cards off the counter, not sounding sorry at all. "Better luck with your books next time."
"Wrong! Wrong! So very wrong!" They cackle, pocketing the coins. "Come back when you've read more."
"Not quite," they say, in a tone that suggests they enjoyed saying it more than they should have. "Try again sometime."
"That's a loss." They shrug theatrically. "The questions are hard on purpose, you know. Wouldn't be fun otherwise."
"Better luck next round," they say, already rearranging the deck. "Or better luck knowing things. Either works."
They shuffle the cards with a flourish. "Another round? Hundred gold, and I promise nothing about the difficulty."
"Feeling smart again?" They fan the deck out. "Hundred gold. Prove it."
"Go again?" They tap the deck against the counter. "Hundred gold. Try not to disappoint me. Or do. I don't actually care."
They rub their hands together. "Back for more? Back for more. You just keep coming back."
The mask stares at you. "I genuinely cannot tell if you're very good or very lucky. Either way." They shrug. "Let's find out."
"You." They point. "You are becoming a problem. A wonderful, profitable — for me — problem."
They count on their fingers, lips moving. Then give up. "You've come back more times than I can count. That's... well, it probably says more about me than you, no?"
"I had to send for reinforcements," they say, entirely sincere. "You'll see."

then.text:
The Gamer Clown goes very still for a moment.

"...I'm impressed," they say. "Genuinely."

They tilt their head.

"You wouldn't like me when I'm surprised."

They split into three.

The new one's mask is frozen mid-gasp — wide eyes, open mouth, arrested mid-shock.

"Get your dice out," they say. The other two agree, very quietly, like this happens often.

The Gamer Clown leans on the counter and looks at you for a long moment.

"You're going to regret trying for a third win." They say it almost fondly.

Then they split.

Three figures, this time: the grinning one, the frowning one, and a third — flat-faced, blank-masked, completely expressionless.

The Serious Clown stares at you.

The other two make noise. You focus on the Serious Clown.

"You might've beaten me once, but that doesn't mean it'll happen again, I'm afraid."

The Gamer Clown steps up onto the booth counter.

"Ready for round two?" They spread their arms wide — and then something happens.

They split.

Two figures drop to either side of the counter: one with a mask fixed in a permanent grin, one with a mask pulled into a long, deep frown.

The Smiley Clown claps their hands. The Frowny Clown sighs.

"Better get your dice out," they say, not quite in unison.

else.text: "For the Trial of Luck," the Gamer Clown announces, climbing atop the booth, "you shall be facing *ME* in the most brutal, the most STRATEGIC, the most cunningly devised game ever conceived by any mind both fevered and brilliant..."

"[wait=0.1]...[wait=0.1]WANDERER'S DICE!"

They produce a set of dice from somewhere and set them on the counter with a flourish.
They gesture at you. "Better get your dice out!"

steps.text: "Well." They stare at the dice. "Well. Look at that." They sweep the coins across the counter toward you. "I don't do that often. Enjoy it while it lasts."
They say nothing for a long moment. Then clap. Once. "Okay. Fine. You win. Take your coins."
"Extraordinary!" They bounce on the spot in an almost forced movement. "Good job! Good job!"
They pull the coins back toward you with two fingers, slowly. "...I'm going to need a minute."
"Fair and square!" They spread their hands. "Can't argue with it. Take your reward — this time."

They sweep the coins off the counter.

"Too bad." The mask grins. "Looks like you just weren't lucky enough."
"Ohhh, so close." They watch the result, unmoved. "The dice decide, not me. Blame the dice."
They lean over the counter. "Bad luck. Pure bad luck." A beat. "Come back and try again." They do not sound sincere, and don't bother pretending otherwise.
"The crowd gives you a solid..." They tilt a hand side to side. "Meeeh. Better luck next time. Maybe."
They pocket the coins without ceremony. "Play more. Get Better. Come back. Lose again. That's the whole pitch, really."
They slap the counter. "Rematch! One hundred gold, right now, I won't even make you wait!"
"Another game?" They're already rearranging the dice. "Hundred gold. Quick as you like."
They stare at you. "...Go again? Hundred gold. Yes or no."
"No?" They look at the empty counter. "Fine. You know where I am when you change your mind."
"Tch." They turn away and start rearranging dice. "Come back when you're feeling generous."
They stare at you. Then wave a hand. "Go. Go on. Come back richer."

steps.text: "Well?" The clown's mask stares at you. "Anything else?"
"What else can I do for you?"
The clown leans back in. "What else?"
The clown taps the counter twice. "Is that all you needed?"
"Anything else?"
